text.title: Python Basics — Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every python-basics application must obey.

rule.statement: Use built-in generic syntax (list[int], dict[str, int], tuple[str, ...]). Do not import typing.List / Dict / Tuple.
rule.rationale: Built-in generics are available since 3.9 and idiomatic since 3.12; typing.List et al. are deprecated aliases that add an import for no benefit.

rule.statement: Use X | None instead of Optional[X]; X | Y instead of Union[X, Y].
rule.rationale: PEP 604 syntax (3.10+) is shorter, requires no typing import, and is the canonical 3.12+ idiom.

rule.statement: Use try/except (EAFP) for exceptional control flow rather than hasattr/in checks before access (LBYL).
rule.rationale: EAFP is Pythonic, faster on the happy path, and avoids TOCTOU races on dynamic attributes.

rule.statement: Never use mutable default arguments (def f(x=[])). Use None and create the container inside the function.
rule.rationale: Default values are evaluated once at function definition; mutations persist across calls and cause invisible cross-test bleed-through.

rule.statement: Use pathlib.Path for file and directory paths. Do not concatenate strings with os.path.join in new code.
rule.rationale: Path is OS-aware, composable with /, and the canonical 3.12+ idiom.

rule.statement: Use @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) for plain data containers. Use NamedTuple only when you need positional indexing.
rule.rationale: Dataclasses give __init__/__repr__/__eq__ for free, frozen=True makes them hashable, slots=True saves memory.
